hook: Britain's only museum of modern Italian art, hidden in a Georgian villa off a leafy square
facts: Housed in Northampton Lodge, built 1807-10 for developer Henry Leroux
Collection assembled by American sociologist Eric Estorick and his wife Salome from 1947
Best known for Futurist works by Boccioni, Balla, Severini and Carra
Opened to the public in January 1998
w: Honeymooning in 1947, an American sociologist and his wife found Boccioni's Futurist manifesto and went straight to buy out a Milanese painter's studio. The drawings and canvases they amassed now hang in a Georgian villa off Canonbury Square: Britain's only home for modern Italian art. Marinetti's speed, framed and quiet.

hook: A 1934 concrete experiment in minimal living that sheltered Bauhaus masters and Soviet spies
facts: Reinforced-concrete block of 36 flats designed by Wells Coates, opened July 1934
Bauhaus emigres Gropius, Breuer and Moholy-Nagy lived here fleeing Nazi Germany
Agatha Christie was a resident between 1941 and 1947
At least four Soviet agents lodged in the flats between 1936 and 1942
w: Concrete liner of a ship, run aground in Hampstead in 1934: 36 tiny flats built for people with few possessions and no time to dust. Gropius and Breuer fled here from the Nazis, Agatha Christie wrote upstairs, and at least four Soviet agents shared the foyer. A Bauhaus dream that doubled as a spy nest.

hook: Queen Anne house from Hampstead's spa boom, now the village's own little museum
facts: Built in 1704 during the reign of Queen Anne
Enlarged in 1720 by Dr William Gibbons, physician to the flourishing Hampstead Wells Spa
Houses the Hampstead Museum, founded by local historians Christopher and Diana Wade
Holds the world's largest collection of work by painter Helen Allingham
w: When Hampstead sold its chalybeate spring water as a cure, the spa doctor moved into this 1704 house and enlarged it. The wells dried up; the bricks stayed. Now it keeps the village's memory: 5,000 objects, a hoard of Helen Allingham watercolours, a panelled music room. Queen Anne brick with a spa-town heart.

n: London Canal Museum & the Ice Well
hook: A Victorian ice warehouse over two vast wells that once held Norwegian ice for ice cream
facts: Warehouse built 1862-63 for Swiss-Italian ice-cream magnate Carlo Gatti
Two wells, each about 30 metres deep, stored ice shipped from Norway by sea and barge
Ice stored here until 1904; the wells were filled in around 1956 and rediscovered in the 1980s
Reckoned the only commercial-scale ice well still viewable in Europe
w: Carlo Gatti taught working London the taste of ice cream, then dug two thirty-metre wells under his canal-side warehouse to hold the Norwegian ice that made it. Filled with rubble in the 1950s and forgotten, one well now gapes open below the floor. The ground around it never thawed. Permafrost under King's Cross.

hook: Islington's oldest building, a Tudor brick tower with a Francis Bacon mystery on the wall
facts: Erected for the canons of St Bartholomew's Priory between 1509 and 1532
Roughly 17 feet square and rising about 60 feet, Grade II* listed
Past occupants include Thomas Cromwell, Francis Bacon and Oliver Goldsmith
Heiress Elizabeth Spencer reputedly eloped from a window in a baker's basket in 1599
legend: The Francis Bacon Society reads a scrubbed-out 'F' beside Elizabeth I on the tower's list of monarchs as a clue to Baconian authorship
w: Sixty feet of Tudor brick, the oldest thing in Islington, raised for the canons before Cromwell, Bacon and Goldsmith took rooms. Legend says an imprisoned heiress was lowered from a window in a baker's basket to elope. On a wall, a name beside Elizabeth I scrubbed to a lone 'F': Baconians swear it names who really wrote Shakespeare.

hook: The terminus where a 1613 artificial river delivered clean water to thirsty London
facts: Opened in 1613 as the London end of the New River, an artificial channel from Hertfordshire
A circular Round Pond fed wooden mains to cisterns across the City
A Smeaton steam engine arrived in 1768 and a Boulton & Watt engine in 1785
Being redeveloped as the Quentin Blake Centre for Illustration
w: From 1613 a hand-cut river ran forty miles out of Hertfordshire to end here in a round brick pond, then trickled into hollow elm pipes feeding the City. Windmills and horse gins pumped it uphill before Smeaton's steam took over. The water industry's birthplace, soon to reopen as a gallery for Quentin Blake's scribbles.

hook: Tudor merchant's house in Waterlow Park, tangled in a Nell Gwynne legend
facts: Originally timber-framed, built for Lord Mayor Richard Martin in 1582
Named for the Earl of Lauderdale, who acquired it through marriage in 1645
Charles II and Samuel Pepys visited in 1666
Stands in Waterlow Park, given to the public in 1889 as 'a garden for the garden-less'
legend: Legend says Nell Gwynne dangled her baby son from a window here until Charles II cried 'God save the Earl of Burford!' to spare him a fall
w: A Lord Mayor raised this timber house in 1582; an earl gave it his name; Pepys came to dine. Legend says Nell Gwynne lodged here and dangled her infant son from an upper window, threatening to drop him until Charles II shouted 'God save the Earl of Burford!' No proof she ever stayed. Good story all the same.

hook: Highgate's old village square, haunted by a half-plucked ghost chicken
facts: Open square at the heart of Highgate village, surrounded by Georgian houses
Named for ponds reputedly dug from gravel pits, later filled in
Sir Francis Bacon is said to have stuffed a fowl with snow nearby in 1626
Bacon caught a chill from the experiment and died days later
legend: They say a half-plucked spectral chicken still flaps shrieking around the square, sighted by a wartime air-raid warden and by a courting couple in the 1970s
w: On a snowy day in 1626 Francis Bacon, struck by a thought about refrigeration, stuffed a chicken with snow somewhere near here, caught a chill, and died of it. The bird, they say, never left. A half-plucked phantom fowl still flaps and shrieks around the square, witnessed by a wartime warden and a startled courting couple.

hook: A roadside stone and a black cat marking where Dick Whittington turned again
facts: Stone tablet erected at the foot of Highgate Hill in 1821, restored 1935
The black cat sculpture by Jonathan Kenworthy added in 1964
Marks where the legendary Whittington heard Bow Bells and turned back
Grade II listed since 1972
legend: Legend says the boy Dick Whittington, fleeing London in despair, heard Bow Bells ring 'Turn again Whittington, thrice Lord Mayor of London' and came back to his fortune
w: A weary kitchen boy quit London and trudged up this hill, only to hear Bow Bells peal 'turn again, thrice Lord Mayor.' He turned. He was made. So runs the legend, marked by an 1821 stone at the foot of Highgate Hill, topped since 1964 by a small black cat in polished limestone. The real Whittington never owned a cat.

hook: Britain's first big Modernist public building, a brick civic landmark in Crouch End
facts: Completed in 1935 to designs by New Zealand-born architect Reginald Uren
Often called the first major UK building in the Modernist style
Won a RIBA bronze medal in 1936 as the best London building of three years
Drew on Dudok's Hilversum and Stockholm town halls
w: In 1935 a young New Zealander gave suburban Crouch End the first serious Modernist building in Britain: slim, pinkish-grey brick, a clean clock tower, Scandinavian calm. RIBA gave it a medal; film crews adopted its Deco interiors. After years dark, it reopened as serviced flats. Civic ambition, faced in beautiful brick.

hook: An 1895 Gothic tower thanking the man who saved Highgate Wood for the public
facts: Erected in 1895 to honour local benefactor Henry Reader Williams
Williams chaired Hornsey's authority and fought to preserve Highgate Wood and Queen's Wood
Designed by architect Frederick Knight, built by Highgate builder J W Dixon
Bronze portrait of Williams sculpted by Alfred Gilbert, maker of Piccadilly's Eros
w: Crouch End's Broadway pivots round a spiky 1895 Gothic tower raised by grateful neighbours for Henry Reader Williams, the council chairman who fought developers to keep Highgate Wood public. His bronze head sits in a niche, carved by Alfred Gilbert, who also made the Eros in Piccadilly. Gratitude, set in stone and clockwork.

hook: A Victorian waterworks dressed as a Scottish castle, now full of climbers
facts: Built 1854-56 as the New River Company's Stoke Newington pumping station
Designed by Robert Billings to resemble a Scottish baronial castle
Constructed after the 1849 cholera outbreak and the Metropolis Water Act
Redundant by 1971, saved by Grade II* listing, reopened as a climbing centre in 1995
w: Cholera frightened Victorian London into cleaning its water, so the New River Company hid a pumping station inside a turreted Scottish castle on Green Lanes, all towers and battlements over steam machinery. The pumps fell silent in 1971; a listing saved the fakery. Climbers now haul up its walls. Industry in fancy dress.

hook: A 17th-century brick cross marking old Tottenham, near the legendary Seven Sisters elms
facts: Built about 1600-09 by Owen Wood on the site of a wooden cross noted in 1409
Stuccoed and Gothicised in 1809; often mistaken for an Eleanor cross
Marks the historic centre of Tottenham village
Near Page Green, where seven elms gave Seven Sisters its name, first recorded 1732
legend: Legend ties the seven elms to seven sisters of the Bruce family, or to a local family of seven sisters who planted them before parting forever
w: An octagonal brick cross of about 1605, Gothicised in stucco and forever mistaken for an Eleanor cross, marks the heart of old Tottenham village. A short walk south stood seven elms ringing a walnut on Page Green. Legend says seven sisters planted them before scattering. Replanted again and again, the ring still holds.

hook: Lubetkin's 1938 health centre that promised free care a decade before the NHS
facts: Designed in 1938 by Tecton, the firm of Berthold Lubetkin
Offered free, progressive medical services ten years before the NHS
Built in a polluted, TB-ridden Finsbury as a light, intimate antidote to grim civic buildings
Embodied Lubetkin's motto 'Nothing is too good for ordinary people'
w: Moscow-trained Lubetkin built this in 1938 for a Finsbury choked with soot and tuberculosis, and gave the poor free, modern medicine a full decade before the NHS existed. Where older clinics loomed Gothic and charged at the door, his glided in glass and light, open and free. 'Nothing is too good for ordinary people.'
